[Belgium](/wiki/Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions, and language areas of Belgium") comprises 565 municipalities ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *gemeenten*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *communes*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Gemeinden*), 285 of them grouped into five [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and 261 others in five provinces in [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), while the remaining 19 are in the [Brussels Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), which is not divided in provinces. In most cases, the municipalities are the smallest administrative subdivisions of Belgium, but in municipalities with more than 100,000 inhabitants, on the initiative of the local council, sub-municipal administrative entities with elected councils may be created. As such, only [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), having over 500,000 inhabitants, became subdivided into [ten districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Antwerp "Districts of Antwerp") ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *districten*).

The Belgian [arrondissements](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_Belgium "Arrondissements of Belgium") ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *arrondissementen*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *arrondissements*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bezirke*), an administrative level between province (or the capital region) and municipality, or the lowest judicial level, are in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") sometimes called districts as well.

## Lists of municipalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Lists of municipalities")]

Here are three lists of municipalities for each one of the three regions:

* [List of municipalities of the Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "List of municipalities of the Brussels-Capital Region") (19 municipalities)
* [List of municipalities of the Flemish Region](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_the_Flemish_Region "List of municipalities of the Flemish Region") (285 municipalities)
* [List of municipalities in Wallonia](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Wallonia "List of municipalities in Wallonia") (261 municipalities)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Before 1830

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Before 1830")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=). *(June 2008)* |

The municipalities, as an [administrative division](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division"), were officially created in 1795, when the [Directoire](/wiki/French_Directory "French Directory") reorganised the structures of the [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime"). The municipalities with less than 5,000 inhabitants were grouped in so-called canton municipalities. In 1800, these canton municipalities were abolished again and the number of autonomous municipalities became 2,776.

Not much changed during the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands"), only a number of smaller municipalities were merged.

### Between 1830 and 1961

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Between 1830 and 1961")]

In 1831, Belgium was divided into 2,739 municipalities, a number which remained more or less constant until 1961. The law of 30 March 1836 regulated municipalities and their governing bodies. The number of municipalities was reduced to 2,508 when the Belgian borders were recognised in 1839, as 124 municipalities were ceded to the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and another 119 municipalities became the [Grand Duchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Luxembourg "Grand Duchy of Luxembourg") (see the article [Communes of Luxembourg](/wiki/Communes_of_Luxembourg "Communes of Luxembourg") for details). New municipalities were created until 1928. There were 2,528 municipalities in 1850, 2,572 in 1875, 2,617 in 1900 and a maximum of 2,675 in 1929. This also includes the municipalities of the [East Cantons](/wiki/Eupen-Malmedy "Eupen-Malmedy") that were added to Belgium following the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War").

### From 1961 to 1977

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: From 1961 to 1977")]

In 1961, the so-called [Unitary Law](/wiki/Unitary_Law "Unitary Law") ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Eenheidswet*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Loi unique*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Einheitsgesetz*), of which the fourth chapter was dedicated to the territorial organisation of the municipalities, was adopted. The authority to abolish municipalities was entrusted to the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") for a period of 10 years. Municipalities could be merged on financial grounds or on grounds of a geographical, linguistic, economic, social or cultural nature. In 1964 and in 1969 and 1970, roughly 300 municipalities ceased to exist and were subsumed into other municipalities. The number of municipalities was reduced from 2,663 in 1961 to 2,586 in 1965 and to 2,359 in 1971.

Article 4 of the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium") states that each municipality must belong to only one of the four official [language areas](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") that were established in 1962–63. In the three officially [unilingual](/wiki/Monolingualism "Monolingualism") language areas, a couple of dozen municipalities in the vicinity of another language area must provide limited facilities for speakers of that other language. As only a law carried by special majorities can change the language status of any municipality, these arrangements have prevented some small [municipalities with facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities") to be [merged](/wiki/Fusion_of_the_communes "Fusion of the communes") in the 1970s, and thus the most minute Belgian municipalities are still found in this group, notably [Herstappe](/wiki/Herstappe "Herstappe") with only 84 inhabitants (in 2006).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Lucien Harmegnies, Minister of the Interior in the government of [Gaston Eyskens](/wiki/Gaston_Eyskens "Gaston Eyskens") (1968–1972) decided to continue the process of territorial reorganization of Belgium. In 1971, the provisions of the Unity Law were extended and modified to apply to large agglomerations, which were initially excluded from its provisions. It was another Minister of the Interior, Joseph Michel, who managed the process. On 30 December 1975 the law regarding the merger of the municipalities was adopted. The merger became effective on 1 January 1977. The [merger of 1977](/wiki/Fusion_of_the_Belgian_municipalities "Fusion of the Belgian municipalities") further reduced the number of municipalities in Belgium from 2,359 to 596.

#### Antwerp

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Antwerp")]

Because of the specific nature of the reorganization in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), the law of 30 December 1975 did not enter into force for Antwerp until 1 January 1983. The formerly-independent municipalities were called [districts](/wiki/Districts "Districts") and were given an advisory function. However, on 1 January 2001 they were given an administrative function again. The merger of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_(district) "Antwerp (district)") with the municipalities of [Berchem](/wiki/Berchem "Berchem"), [Borgerhout](/wiki/Borgerhout "Borgerhout"), [Deurne](/wiki/Deurne,_Belgium "Deurne, Belgium"), [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_Antwerp "Hoboken, Antwerp"), [Ekeren](/wiki/Ekeren "Ekeren"), [Merksem](/wiki/Merksem "Merksem") and [Wilrijk](/wiki/Wilrijk "Wilrijk") in 1983 finally reduced the number of municipalities in Belgium to 589 and was the last reorganization of the municipalities for several decades because the merger of the 19 municipalities of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") was postponed indefinitely.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 21st century")]

The fifth [state reform](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") (2001) transferred the responsibility over municipalities from the federal level to the three regions.

This did not instantly have any significant effect on the reorganisation of municipalities, up until the Flemish [Bourgeois Government](/wiki/Bourgeois_Government "Bourgeois Government") (2014-2019) provided a legal framework and financial incentives for municipalities to consider merging. This led 15 Flemish municipalities to merge into seven, decreasing the total number of Flemish municipalities from 308 to 300. Their municipal councils were elected in the [regular elections of 14 October 2018](/wiki/2018_Belgian_local_elections "2018 Belgian local elections"), and the change took effect on 1 January 2019.

## Municipal organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Municipal organization")]

### Mayor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mayor")]

The mayor ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *burgemeester*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *bourgmestre*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bürgermeister*) is not only the head of the municipality but also the representative of the [regional](/wiki/Communities_and_regions_of_Belgium "Communities and regions of Belgium") and the [federal government](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Government "Belgian Federal Government") at the local level. In that capacity, they are responsible for the execution of [laws](/wiki/Law "Law"), [decrees](/wiki/Decree "Decree"), [ordinances](/wiki/Ordinance_(Belgium) "Ordinance (Belgium)") and orders. The mayor is also responsible for the maintenance of public order in their municipality. They chair the college of mayor and aldermen or the municipal college, depending on the [region](/wiki/Communities_and_regions_of_Belgium "Communities and regions of Belgium"), as well.

In the [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), the mayor is appointed by the regional government, on the nomination of the municipal council, for a term of office of six years. In [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), the mayor is the municipal councillor who received the largest number of preferential votes of the majority party that received the largest number of votes in the municipal elections. Hence, it is also possible that the mayor is not a member of the largest party, as the largest party is not always part of the governing coalition. It is also possible in Wallonia for the municipal council to adopt a constructive [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") in the municipal college.

### College

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: College")]

The executive organ of the municipality is known as the college of mayor and aldermen ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *college van burgemeester en schepenen*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *collège des bourgmestre et échevins*), commonly referred to as the college of aldermen ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *schepencollege*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *collège échevinal*), in Flanders and Brussels, and as the municipal college ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *collège communal*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *gemeindekollegium*) in Wallonia. This college is responsible for the daily administration of the municipality. It is also responsible for the preparation and implementation of the decisions of the municipal council.

### Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Council")]

The municipal council ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *gemeenteraad*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *conseil communal*; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Gemeinderat*) is the representative assembly of the municipality and consists of members directly elected for a term of office of six years. The number of municipal councillors depends on the number of inhabitants of the municipality, and can vary from 7 to 55. It is responsible for all matters that are of municipal interest.

## Differences between the regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Differences between the regions")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=). *(June 2008)* |

Following the [Fifth State Reform](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") in 2001, the responsibility for the composition, the organization, the competences and the activities of the municipal institutions were devolved to the [regions](/wiki/Communities_and_regions_of_Belgium "Communities and regions of Belgium"), as well as the responsibility for the [provincial institutions](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium"). As a result, there are several differences between the municipal institutions in Flanders, in Wallonia and in Brussels. Wallonia has also further devolved part of its responsibilities to the [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium") with regard to its 9 municipalities.

The three regions can amend or replace the existing legislation on the municipalities, most notably the New Municipal Law. In the [Flanders](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"), the Municipal Decree of 15 July 2005 applies. In Wallonia the Code of Local Democracy and Decentralization applies. In Brussels several provisions of the New Municipal Law have been modified by [ordinance](/wiki/Ordinance_(Belgium) "Ordinance (Belgium)"), such as the Ordinance of 17 July 2003. The legal framework in the three regions is still relatively similar, but that could change in the future.

## Agglomerations and federations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Agglomerations and federations")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=). *(June 2008)* |

Since 1970, the [Belgian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium") includes the possibility to create [agglomerations](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration "Urban agglomeration") and federations of municipalities by law. This possibility was only used once in 1971 when the Brussels Agglomeration, comprising the 19 municipalities of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), was put into place. It *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* ceased to exist in 1989 when the organs of the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region") were established. **The only election ever held for the Brussels Agglomeration council took place on 21 November 1971**[[2]](#cite_note-2), reflecting the limited use of this constitutional institution before it was replaced by regional bodies.
